name: Torcal Alto Ascent Trail
name_original: Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera – circular al Camorro Alto
description: Tackle the Torcal Alto Ascent Trail, a difficult 6.0-mile jogging route through a surreal karst landscape with 1233 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Jogging around Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera offers an exploration of its distinctive karst landscape, characterized by unique limestone formations sculpted over millions of years. The region features an "otherworldly" terrain with natural rock towers and winding paths, providing a visually engaging experience for runners. Elevation changes and varied geology contribute to a dynamic environment. This UNESCO World Heritage site presents a network of trails suitable for different fitness levels. Best jogging routes around Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera The most popular jogging route is Torcal Alto Ascent Trail, a 6.0 miles (9.7 km) trail that takes 1 hour 22 minutes to complete. This difficult route provides a challenging ascent through the unique geological formations. Another top favourite among local runners is Plazoleta Cuatro Torres – The Torcal of Antequera loop from Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera, a difficult 1.8 miles (2.8 km) path. This shorter loop offers an immersive experience within the sculpted limestone landscape. Local runners also love the Running loop from La Posada del Torcal, a 6.3 miles (10.1 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 1 hour 7 minutes. Jogging around Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera is defined by its extraordinary karst landscape, unique limestone formations, and diverse natural features. The network of running routes offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 6 reviews. More than 500 runners have used komoot to explore Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera?
A: There are over 50 running routes available in Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera, offering a wide range of options for different fitness levels and preferences.

Q: What do other runners appreciate most about the running trails in Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera?
A: The running routes in Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 6 reviews. Runners often praise the unique, 'otherworldly' karst landscape and the visually engaging experience provided by the sculpted limestone formations.

Q: Are there easy running routes for beginners in El Torcal?
A: While many routes in El Torcal are considered moderate to difficult due to the varied terrain and elevation changes, there are a few easier options. For example, the Green Route (1.5 km) is described as medium-low difficulty, though its terrain can be very rocky and irregular. It's always recommended to check the route details before heading out.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the jogging routes in El Torcal?
A: The terrain in El Torcal is predominantly characterized by its extraordinary karst landscape, meaning you'll encounter unique limestone formations and winding paths. Some trails, like the Green Route, can be very rocky and irregular. However, there are also options like the 'Ruta Naranja' (Orange Route), which offers a challenging, steep asphalt ascent from the lower to the upper parking lot, providing a consistent surface.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in the area?
A: Yes, there are several circular running routes available. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38151795" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plazoleta Cuatro Torres – The Torcal of Antequera loop from Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera&lt;/a&gt;, which is a difficult 2.8 km path offering an immersive experience within the sculpted limestone landscape. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40007930" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Running loop from La Posada del Torcal&lt;/a&gt;, a 10.1 km trail leading through varied terrain.

Q: How long do the running trails typically take to complete?
A: The duration of running trails in El Torcal varies significantly depending on the route's length and difficulty. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996271305/torcal-alto-ascent-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torcal Alto Ascent Trail&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 9.7 km route, typically takes about 1 hour 22 minutes. Shorter loops like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38151795" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plazoleta Cuatro Torres – The Torcal of Antequera loop&lt;/a&gt; (2.8 km) can be completed in around 24 minutes, while longer, more challenging routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38153204" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campsite Near El Torcal – Río de la Villa Spring loop&lt;/a&gt; (16.5 km) might take over 2 hours.

Q: What natural landmarks or viewpoints can I see while running in El Torcal?
A: While running in El Torcal, you'll encounter numerous unique geological formations. Notable landmarks include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/230042" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Torcal of Antequera&lt;/a&gt; itself, and specific formations like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3529822" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Cathedrals of El Torcal&lt;/a&gt;. The entire area is a 'museum of natural sculptures,' offering an 'otherworldly' experience with its rock towers and winding paths.

Q: Is there parking available for runners at Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera?
A: Yes, parking is available. The Torcal Alto visitor center serves as a starting point for various trails and has parking facilities. However, during weekends and holidays, private vehicle access to the upper parking lot may be restricted, with a shuttle bus service available from a lower parking area.

Q: How can I reach the running trails in El Torcal using public transport?
A: While direct public transport to the trailheads might be limited, a shuttle bus service is often available, especially during peak times, to transport visitors from a lower parking area to the Torcal Alto visitor center, which is the starting point for many trails. It's advisable to check local transport schedules before your visit.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails in Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera?
A: El Torcal de Antequera is a protected natural area with diverse flora and fauna. While specific regulations for dogs on all trails can vary, it's generally recommended to keep dogs on a leash and adhere to park rules to protect the wildlife and natural environment. Always check the latest park guidelines before bringing your pet.

Q: Are the running routes in El Torcal suitable for families?
A: Some routes in El Torcal are suitable for families, particularly those looking for a unique outdoor experience. The Green Route (1.5 km) is considered medium-low difficulty, making it an option for families, though its rocky and irregular terrain should be noted. The 'otherworldly' landscape can be very engaging for children. Always consider the fitness level of all family members before choosing a route.

Q: When is the best time of year to go running in Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera?
A: The best time to visit El Torcal for running often depends on personal preference and weather conditions. Spring and autumn generally offer milder temperatures, which are ideal for outdoor activities. Summers can be hot, while winters can be cooler, so checking the weather forecast is always recommended.

Q: Are there any facilities like cafes near the running routes?
A: Yes, the Torcal Alto visitor center, which serves as a starting point for many trails, typically offers facilities including information services. While not explicitly listed as cafes directly on the routes, visitor centers in natural parks often have amenities where you can get refreshments.

Q: What are some challenging running routes for experienced runners?
A: Experienced runners looking for a challenge will find several difficult routes in El Torcal. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996271305/torcal-alto-ascent-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torcal Alto Ascent Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 9.7 km route with significant elevation gain. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31432028" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Descent to Antequera – Descent into Antequera loop from Restaurante Molino Blanco&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 11 km trail with considerable elevation changes.
tips: The parking lot of the old Venta del Rosario is a good option when the parking lot of the Visitor Center is full, taking advantage of the shuttle bus (€2) of the protected natural area to access the interior of the Torcal Alto. On this occasion the start of the trail is the car park, which is about 50 meters after passing the entrance to the torcal, along the signposted route Ascent to Torcal Alto or the orange route. The path is unmissable, it follows old cattle paths and shepherd paths through the Cañada de Tosaires. On the way up there is a panoramic viewpoint towards the mountains of Villanueva de la Concepción and the mountains of Málaga. And shortly after, an old shepherd's hut or formazo, is a traditional stone construction with a vegetable roof used as a shelter by the ranchers of the mountains until the forties of the last century.

The climb ends at the edge of the Torcal Alto, a fantastic karstic plateau known as Camorro de las Siete Mesas. The layout of the path is clear in this section, it passes through El Sombrero, also called El Platillo in some publications, and shortly after a fork appears. The trail turns to the left and moves away from the cliffs to pass near the summit of Camorro Alto. One option is to continue straight ahead along the ledge of the Cornisa del Diablo, the views and atmosphere are spectacular but there are a couple of exposed steps and a small climb (II) through a channel to get off the ledge. The trail runs through the karstic plateau of the Camorro de las Siete Mesas and turns off again towards the edge of the cliffs to enjoy a fantastic view of the Torcal Bajo and the Antequera plain. The best option is to return back to the main trail and continue west along the old red route. At some points stone landmarks appear.

faqs.question: Is the Torcal Alto Ascent Trail suitable for jogging?
faqs.answer: No, this trail is generally not ideal for jogging. The terrain is very rocky, irregular, and often covered with loose stones, making it uneven and potentially slippery, especially after rain. While some experienced trail runners might attempt it, it's not recommended for casual jogging due to the technical nature of the path and the significant ascent.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this trail?
faqs.answer: The terrain is consistently very rocky and irregular. You'll encounter paths covered with stones and rocks, making for an uneven surface. After rain, sections can become muddy and very slippery. Proper, sturdy footwear, preferably ankle-high hiking boots, is highly recommended for navigating the rugged ground and loose stones.

faqs.question: Where can I park and access the trailhead?
faqs.answer: The trail typically starts from the lower parking lot, which is near Venta del Rosario. This is the starting point for the 'Subida al Torcal Alto' (Orange Route) which leads up to the Visitor Center.

faqs.question: Is the trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: The general policy for El Torcal de Antequera Natural Park often restricts dogs, especially on certain routes or requires them to be on a leash. It's best to check the latest regulations directly with the park authorities before planning your visit with a dog.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to visit El Torcal de Antequera?
faqs.answer: The best times to visit are generally spring and autumn when temperatures are milder. Summers can be very hot, and winters can be cold, with potential for ice or snow at higher elevations, which can make the rocky terrain even more challenging.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees for the Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera?
faqs.answer: Access to the Paraje Natural Torcal de Antequera and its trails, including the Torcal Alto Ascent Trail, is generally free of charge. However, it's always a good idea to check the official park website for any recent changes or specific regulations regarding guided tours or special activities.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You can expect a truly unique and 'surrealistic' landscape of bizarre limestone formations, often described as a 'forest of rocks'. These ancient karst structures, sculpted by millions of years of erosion, create an otherworldly environment. You'll also find diverse flora and panoramic views of the surrounding valleys.

faqs.question: Are there any notable viewpoints or landmarks on the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the area is famous for its imaginatively named rock formations like 'El Dedo,' 'El Camello,' 'La Jarra y La Botella,' 'La Esfinge,' and the 'El Tornillo' Natural Monument. You can also enjoy panoramic views from spots like Mirador Las Ventanillas, offering vistas of the valleys and sometimes even the Mediterranean Sea.

faqs.question: What wildlife might I encounter?
faqs.answer: The park is home to diverse wildlife, with wild ibexes being a notable species you might spot. Keep an eye out for various bird species and other small mammals that inhabit this unique ecosystem.

faqs.question: Is this a loop trail or an out-and-back route?
faqs.answer: The specific 'Torcal Alto Ascent Trail' (often referred to as the Orange Route) is primarily a linear ascent from the lower parking lot to the Visitor Center. However, the komoot tour provided is a circular route that includes this ascent and continues to Camorro Alto, making the overall experience a loop.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Subida al Torcal Alto and the [CIMA ML09] Torcal de Antequera * Antequera trails.
